# Charli.info

> Charli.info is the public home of Charlotte Joanne Tyrer's CharliVerse work. It introduces the ecosystem, its accessibility commitments, and the projects, publications, and communities that sit inside it. Content is licensed under CC BY-SA 4.0 unless stated otherwise.

## Home

We have equality.

Blind people have the same relationship with technology as everyone else in 2026.

What Work Looks Like Now

I've been thinking about the first three months of this year. I use AI a lot - maybe not 996, but after two mugs of coffee and feeding my dragons, I open my laptop and that tends to be me for the next twelve hours.

There have been a number of articles and podcasts lately talking about AI burnout, AI brain rot, and the latest: AI brain fry. This latest can be described as people having so much to do that they want to do even more. I think it's completely made up. I myself have nothing more than:

1. A part-time degree in Philosophy and Psychology to complete.
2. A three-to-six month plan to develop and launch Red DOG Studio - an autonomous NVDA add-on creation process.
3. A ten-year plan to found a Museum of Access and Assistive Technology.
4. A LinkedIn newsletter.
5. A Substack newsletter.
6. Blind Ant, a CharliVerse Lived Experience Validation studio.
7. And last, but not least, an online community of more than a thousand blind AI enthusiasts to help shepherd.

From Tinker Tax to Play

The truth is that this isn't overload - it's the reality of working with AI in 2026. When the tools actually work, one person can operate across spaces that previously required teams, departments, and budgets. Writing, analysis, design, community building, prototyping - the barriers between them have largely disappeared. What used to be separate roles now collapse into a single flow of curiosity and execution.

For me, the real change is something simpler. For most of my life, doing anything with technology meant paying what I call the tinker tax - the hidden labour of making tools work at all. Fighting inaccessible interfaces, stitching together workflows, persuading software to behave long before you could actually start the real task. AI changes that equation. When the tools finally meet you halfway, the energy that used to be spent wrestling the machinery is suddenly available for thinking.

And when that happens, something unexpected appears. What looks like "a lot of work" from the outside doesn't feel like work from the inside. It feels like play. Ideas lead to experiments, experiments lead to posts, posts lead to conversations, conversations lead to new projects. It's not a workload so much as a landscape you get to wander through. For the first time in a long time, the technology isn't the obstacle. It's the thing that lets the mind run free.

## The CharliVerse

The CharliVerse is the name for the interconnected body of work I operate across - studios, publications, communities, and frameworks that share a common set of ideas about AI, access, and what it means to think carefully in public.

It is run by one person, directed by one person, and held together by a coherent intellectual framework rather than an organisational chart. The tools that make this possible are AI agents. The philosophy that shapes it is my own.

### Studios and projects

#### Blind Ant

A lived experience validation studio operating at the intersection of AI and accessibility. Blind Ant tests, interrogates, and validates AI tools and outputs from the perspective of a blind practitioner with decades of experience in access technology.

https://blindant.co.uk

#### Museum of Access and Assistive Technology

A museum founded on the principle that testimony matters more than objects. The Museum of Access and Assistive Technology documents the lived experience of using assistive technology - the breakthroughs, the failures, and the ordinary daily reality. Planned soft opening summer 2026.

https://accessassistive.org

#### CARRADOS

Home of the Tyrer Framework - the body of theoretical work underpinning the CharliVerse, including Tyrer's Law, the Tyrer Accessibility Scale, and the Inference Space model.

https://carrados.co.uk

### Publications and communities

#### Postcards from Inference Space

A Substack publication for AI practitioners. Writing about working with AI at the frontier - inference engineering, agent architecture, and what it actually feels like to spend thousands of hours inside these systems.

https://inferenceSpace.substack.com

#### Through the AIs of the Blind

A LinkedIn newsletter covering AI from a blind perspective. Practical, honest, and grounded in lived experience.

https://www.linkedin.com/newsletters/through-the-ais-of-the-blind-7384240304901689344/

#### AI with the Blind

A Facebook community of more than 1,200 blind AI enthusiasts. The largest dedicated space for blind people working with AI tools.

https://fb.me/AIWithTheBlind

## Accessibility Statement

Charli.info follows a Zero Information Casualty baseline. Information must remain available without relying on sight, precision pointing, motion tolerance, or default visual settings.

### What This Site Commits To

- All core content is available as real text in semantic HTML.
- Every page provides a skip link, a main landmark, clear headings, and keyboard-visible focus.
- The site respects operating system preferences for reduced motion, color scheme, and higher contrast.
- Links remain identifiable without relying on color alone.
- Metadata is present for page titles, descriptions, canonical URLs, and structured data.
- If images are added, they must include WCAG-compliant alternative text unless they are purely decorative.

### Current Scope

The current public site is intentionally simple: three public pages, shared navigation, and one shared stylesheet. That simplicity is part of the accessibility strategy.

### Verification

The site is checked with Astro project diagnostics and a repo-local ZIC verification script against the built HTML output.

### Feedback

If any part of the site becomes hard to navigate or understand with assistive technology, that is a defect rather than an edge case.

## Source and licence
